content:
Chinese researchers have successfully demonstrated the world’s first field trial of a hollow-core fiber transmission system capable of delivering 1.2Tb/s per wavelength.

The team achieved a total transmission capacity of 51.3Tb/s over a distance of 128 miles, or 206 km.

The test was carried out on the world’s longest commercial cross-border hollow-core fiber link without using signal repeaters.

Unlike normal optical fiber, hollow-core fiber sends light through air instead of glass.

This helps reduce latency and allows much higher data transmission capacity.

Researchers also developed a new high-power optical amplifier and an adaptive wavelength-control system.

These technologies helped the system maintain stable and high-speed performance in real-world conditions.

The breakthrough could help improve future AI networks, cloud computing systems, and data center connections.

It may also increase fiber network capacity without needing to fully replace existing infrastructure.

Disclaimer: This post is for informational purposes only and is based on publicly available reports. The image is AI generated and is just for reference.

comment: Interesting, but real deployment hurdle is in the field: splicing hollow-core fiber with legacy glass networks and added connector losses may offset latency gains in metro rollouts.
